---
title: "Getting Started with Claude Code"
subtitle: "A practical guide for IPA Colombia Research Analysts"
author: "IPA Colombia MEL Team"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-copy: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    css: styles.css
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-banner">
  <div class="hero-content">
    <p class="hero-eyebrow">IPA Colombia &middot; 50–75 min</p>
    <h1 class="hero-title">Learn Claude Code<br>for Stata Research</h1>
    <p class="hero-subtitle">
      Go from zero to productive with an AI coding assistant.<br>
      No prior experience with VS Code or Claude Code required.
    </p>
    <div class="hero-badges">
      <span class="badge-item">📖 5 Modules</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🔒 PII Safety</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🎯 2 Exercises</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.module-nav}
This guide has five modules. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need. Each module is self-contained.
:::

::: {.panel-tabset .module-tabs}

## 1. Setup

### What You Need Before Starting

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Must Have
- Stata 17 or later, installed and licensed
- Node.js 18 or later ([download](https://nodejs.org/))
- An Anthropic API key **or** a Claude Max/Pro account
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Good to Have
- A GitHub account ([sign up](https://github.com/))
- A project repository cloned to your machine
- Familiarity with IPA/DIME Stata coding standards
:::
::::
:::::

---

### What Is GitHub?

GitHub is a platform where teams store and collaborate on code. Think of it as a shared folder with superpowers: it keeps a complete history of every change, lets multiple people work on the same files without overwriting each other, and provides a backup of all project code outside your local machine.

At IPA, each project has a **repository** (repo) on GitHub. A repository is a folder that contains all the do-files, documentation, and configuration for one project. The code lives on GitHub, and each analyst **clones** (downloads) a copy to their own computer to work on.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Repository
A project folder on GitHub. Contains do-files, documentation, and configs. Each project gets one repo.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Clone
Download a copy of the repo to your computer. You edit your local copy, then push changes back.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Commit & Push
Save a snapshot of your changes (commit) and upload them to GitHub (push) so the team can see them.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
You do not need to be a Git expert. The three concepts above (repo, clone, commit/push) are enough to get started. Claude Code can help with Git commands when you need them.
:::

---

### Project Folder Structure

IPA projects should follow a standardized folder structure. The official template is available at [PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template](https://github.com/PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template) on GitHub.

To start a new project, go to that repository and click **"Use this template"** to create your own copy. This gives you:

```
my-project/
├── do_files/           # Stata scripts
│   └── 00_run.do       # Master do-file (runs everything)
├── data/               # Raw, cleaned, and final datasets
├── outputs/            # Generated tables and figures
│   ├── tables/
│   └── figures/
├── logs/               # Stata execution logs
├── ado/                # Local Stata packages
├── documentation/      # Project guides and references
├── .config/            # Configuration files
├── .env                # Local Stata path (not committed to Git)
└── .here               # Root marker for automatic path detection
```

The template supports three tiers of complexity:

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Tier 1: Minimal
Git + Stata only. Clone the template, configure `.env` with your Stata path, and start writing do-files.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Tier 2: Task Runner
Add `just` for automation. Run scripts with `just stata-run` instead of opening Stata manually.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Tier 3: Full
Python tools, pre-commit hooks, and Claude Code integration with `CLAUDE.md` and skills.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
If you already have a project repo, you do not need to start over. The folder structure above is a reference. Ask your project lead how your repo is organized.
:::

---

### VS Code: Your New Workspace

Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free code editor where Claude Code runs. Download it from [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com/).

Most research analysts switch between the Stata graphical user interface (GUI), a file browser, and a terminal window. VS Code puts all three into one screen.

**The four panels you need:**

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Sidebar
Shows your project folder structure. Toggle with `Ctrl+B`. Click any file to open it.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Editor
The main area where files open as tabs. Split the view to compare two files side by side.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Terminal
A command line built into VS Code. This is where Claude Code runs. Toggle with `` Ctrl+` ``.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Extensions
A marketplace for add-ons. Open with `Ctrl+Shift+X`.
:::
::::
:::::

**Three essential shortcuts:**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Ctrl+K`, then `Ctrl+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Ctrl+Shift+F` |

#### macOS

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Cmd+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Cmd+Shift+F` |
:::

**Extensions to install** (run in the VS Code terminal):

```bash
code --install-extension kylebarron.stata-enhanced
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
```

---

### Install Claude Code

Open the VS Code terminal (`` Ctrl+` ``) and run:

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Permissions error on Windows? Close VS Code and reopen as Administrator. On macOS, prefix with `sudo`.
:::

### Authenticate

There are two ways to use Claude Code. Choose the one that applies to you.

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### API Key (Pay Per Use)

An API key charges per use. This is the standard setup for IPA projects where the team shares a billing account. Ask your project lead for the key.

Set it as an environment variable:

::: {.panel-tabset}
##### Windows (PowerShell)

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY", "sk-ant-your-key-here", "User")
```

Restart VS Code after running this command.

##### macOS / Linux

Add to your `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"
```

Then restart your terminal.
:::

#### Claude Account (Max / Pro)

If you have a Claude Max or Pro subscription, you can use Claude Code without a separate API key.

Launch Claude Code (see below) and follow the browser login prompt. It opens a window where you sign in with your Claude credentials. No environment variable needed. The session token is stored locally after the first login.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Keep credentials private.** Never paste your API key into a shared document, Slack message, or Git commit. If using a Claude account, do not share your login session.
:::

### Launch and Verify

Navigate to your project folder and start Claude Code:

```bash
cd /path/to/your/project
claude
```

To verify it works, type:

```
What project is this?
```

Claude Code reads the `CLAUDE.md` file on startup and should describe your project. If it does, you are ready.


## 2. Concepts

### What Is Claude Code?

Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built by Anthropic. It runs inside the VS Code terminal as a command-line tool. Unlike browser-based chatbots, it reads the files in your project folder, understands the structure, and can write or edit code directly on your machine.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 🗂️ Project Context
Reads your files automatically. Sees do-files, configs, and docs. No copy-pasting into a chat window.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### ✏️ Direct Editing
Creates, modifies, and deletes files in your project. Asks for your approval before making changes.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 📋 Instructions
Follows rules in a `CLAUDE.md` file at your project root. Coding standards, conventions, and guardrails.
:::
::::
:::::

---

### What Claude Code Can and Cannot Do

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-safe}
### Can Do
- Write new do-files following IPA/DIME standards
- Debug errors by reading Stata log files
- Generate LaTeX tables with `esttab` or `outreg2`
- Explain existing code in plain language
- Review do-files for coding standard violations
- Help with Git commands
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Cannot Do
- Access or read data containing PII
- Replace your judgment on research design
- Run Stata by itself (uses `just` commands)
- Access Box, email, or external systems
- Guarantee correct statistical methodology
- Remember across separate sessions
:::
::::
:::::

---

### How Claude Code Knows Your Project

Claude Code does not start from zero each time. Three mechanisms give it persistent knowledge.

**CLAUDE.md: The project briefing.** Think of it as the document you would write for a new research analyst on their first day. It tells Claude Code what the project is, what rules to follow, and where to find things.

```yaml
# CLAUDE.md — SpC Project

## What This Project Is
SpC 3.0 = Sanar para Crecer 3.0 (HtG)
A large-scale caregiver education program implemented by ICBF
in Colombia. The program includes an embedded RCT.

## Project-Specific Rules
- PII: Raw data contains PII — never read or display raw data files.
- Do not use cap / capture except for cap mkdir.
- Path globals are defined in 0_paths.do. Never redefine them.
```

This file is checked into Git. Every team member shares the same instructions.

**Skills: The reference manuals.** The `.claude/skills/` directory contains specialized instruction sets with rules, examples, and conventions.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Stata Skill
IPA/DIME coding standards, do-file headers, path conventions, missing value handling, four-stage data flow.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### IPA Writing Skill
Style rules for IPA content: paragraph structure, active voice, acronym conventions, citation format.
:::
::::
:::::

Claude Code loads the relevant skill automatically. Ask it to write a do-file and it applies the Stata skill. Ask it to draft report text and it applies the writing guidelines.

> **Mental model:** `CLAUDE.md` is the briefing for a new RA. Skills are the reference manuals on their desk.

**Settings and permissions.** The `.claude/settings.json` file controls which commands Claude Code can run without asking. You do not need to modify these files. They are maintained by the project lead.


## 3. PII Safety

:::{.callout-danger appearance="simple"}
**This is the most important module in the guide.** Read it before using Claude Code on any project with human subjects data.
:::

Innovations for Poverty Action (IPA) projects handle data with personally identifiable information (PII): names, national identification numbers (cedula), addresses, and phone numbers. Claude Code can read any file in your VS Code workspace and sends your messages to external servers for processing.

### The Four Rules

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 1: No PII in Prompts
Do not type or paste names, cedula numbers, addresses, or phone numbers into Claude Code. Messages are sent to Anthropic's servers.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 2: No Raw Data Files
Never ask Claude Code to open files in `data/raw/` or the encrypted vault (D: drive). Work only with do-files, logs, and de-identified outputs.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 3: No Individual-Level Output
Do not ask for rows of data. Even de-identified data can become re-identifiable. Ask it to write code that produces summaries instead.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 4: Review Before Committing
Check every file Claude Code creates or modifies. Confirm no PII leaked into comments, labels, or hard-coded values.
:::
::::
:::::

### Safe vs. Unsafe

> Before sending a prompt, ask yourself: "Does this require Claude Code to see individual-level data or personal details?" If yes, rephrase to focus on the **code**, not the **data**.

| Scenario | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| "Write a do-file that cleans survey data following this structure" | ✅ Safe |
| "Clean this survey data" | ❌ Unsafe |
| "Why is my merge failing? Here is the log" | ✅ Safe |
| "Show me the first 20 rows of the baseline dataset" | ❌ Unsafe |
| "Debug this r(111) error in my do-file" | ✅ Safe |
| "Summarize attendance for Maria Garcia in Centro Zonal Norte" | ❌ Unsafe |


## 4. Prompting

### Five Patterns for Effective Prompts

These patterns, each with a weak and strong version, will help you get useful results on the first try.

---

**Pattern 1: Describe the output, not the steps**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Use regress, then outreg2, then add a title, then save as .tex
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Run an ITT regression of FFMQ on treatment with strata fixed effects
and SEs clustered at the UDS level. Export to LaTeX using the outreg2
conventions in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
```
:::

---

**Pattern 2: Reference project conventions**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Make a nice table with the results
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Export using the esttab pattern in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
Match the column structure of the attrition tables in outputs/tables/.
```
:::

---

**Pattern 3: Provide context about errors**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
I got r(111), please fix
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
I ran `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis` and got r(111).
Here are the last 30 lines of the log:

[paste log excerpt]

The error is on the line referencing `treatment_status`.
I think the variable was renamed upstream.
```
:::

---

**Pattern 4: Ask for explanations alongside code**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Write the merge command
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Write the merge command and explain why you chose 1:1 vs m:1.
Also explain what the assert results mean.
```
:::

---

**Pattern 5: Iterate, do not start over**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
[rewrites the entire prompt from scratch]
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
The table looks right. Add baseline controls as a second column
and include the F-test for joint significance at the bottom.
```
:::

Claude Code remembers the conversation. Build on its output rather than starting over each time.


## 5. Exercises

### Exercise 1: Debug a Stata Error

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on** · 10–15 minutes
:::

**Scenario:** You ran an attrition analysis script and the log shows this error:

```stata
. use "$data/clean/masterMUESTRA_final_RCT.dta", clear

. gen attrited = (endline_survey == 0)

. regress attrited treatment_status i.strata, vce(cluster cod_uds)
variable treatment_status not found
r(111);

end of do-file
r(111);
```

**Your task:**

1. Open Claude Code in your project folder (`claude` in the terminal)
2. Paste the log and ask: *"I got this r(111) error running my attrition analysis. What is wrong and how do I fix it?"*
3. Read the diagnosis. Does it identify `r(111)` as "variable not found"?
4. Follow up: *"Check what treatment-related variables exist in the do-files."*
5. Evaluate the proposed fix

**What to look for:**

- Does it identify the correct error type?
- Does it suggest checking existing do-files for the real variable name?
- Does the fix follow conventions (no `capture`, proper path globals)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal Answer

The variable is named `treatment` (or `treat`), not `treatment_status`. This happens when variable names change during cleaning but downstream scripts are not updated. The fix is a single-line change, not a workaround with `capture` or `rename`.
:::

---

### Exercise 2: Write a Summary Statistics Do-File

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on** · 15–20 minutes
:::

**Scenario:** Your project lead asks for a summary statistics table of early childhood development (ECD) worker outcomes at endline, by treatment group.

**Step 1: Write the prompt.** Use this specification (remember Pattern 1):

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
**Variables:**

- `ffmq_total` (mindfulness)
- `dass_stress` (stress)
- `dass_anxiety` (anxiety)
- `dass_depression` (depression)
- `psi_total` (parenting stress)
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
**Requirements:**

- Breakdown by treatment arm (0/1)
- Mean, SD, N, and t-test p-value
- LaTeX output via `esttab`
- Follow `CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md`
::::
:::::

**Step 2: Review the output** against this checklist:

- [ ] Proper header (project, purpose, author, date)
- [ ] Path globals (`$data`, `$tables`) instead of hard-coded paths
- [ ] No `capture` (except `cap mkdir`)
- [ ] Extended missing values handled (`.d`, `.n`, `.r`, `.s`)
- [ ] Clean `esttab` producing LaTeX output
- [ ] Saved to `$tables` directory

**Step 3: Iterate.** Ask Claude Code for two changes:

1. *"Add a column with the treatment-control difference and the t-test p-value."*
2. *"Exclude `.d` (do not know) and `.r` (refused) from calculations but keep `.n` (not applicable) observations."*

**Step 4: Evaluate.** Did it modify the do-file without breaking the existing structure?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal: Strong Output

- Clean header with project metadata
- Path globals instead of hard-coded paths
- A loop over outcome variables (avoids repetition)
- `eststo` and `esttab` used together
- Missing values handled with `if !inlist(var, .d, .r)`
- Output saved as `.tex` and `.csv`
- No `capture` commands
- Comments explain *why*, not just *what*
:::

---

### Quick Reference

**Common interactions:**

| What You Want | What to Type |
|---------------|-------------|
| Start Claude Code | `claude` in the terminal |
| Edit a file | *"Edit [path] to [change]"* |
| Create a file | *"Create a do-file that [spec]"* |
| Run Stata | *"Run `just stata-script mel/2_AttritionAnalysis`"* |
| Debug an error | Paste the log and ask *"What is wrong?"* |
| Exit | `/exit` or `Ctrl+C` |

**Safe vs. unsafe at a glance:**

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-safe}
### ✅ Safe
- "Write a do-file that..."
- "Debug this log output..."
- "Explain this Stata command..."
- "Generate a LaTeX table for..."
- "Review this do-file for errors..."
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### ❌ Never
- "Read the raw survey data..."
- "Show me observations where name =..."
- "Summarize the PII variables..."
- "List all respondent names..."
- "Open the file on D: drive..."
:::
::::
:::::

**Next steps after this guide:**

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟢 Start Here
Ask Claude Code to **explain** an unfamiliar section of an existing do-file.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🔵 Then Try
Paste a **Stata error** from a log and ask for a diagnosis.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟣 Build Up
Ask Claude Code to **review** a do-file for coding standard violations.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟠 Go Further
Generate a **summary statistics table** for a presentation.
:::
::::
:::::

**Resources:**

- [Claude Code documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)
- [IPA Stata template](https://github.com/PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template)
- Your project's `CLAUDE.md` and `.claude/skills/` directory

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Claude Code is a tool, not a replacement for analytical judgment. Always review code it generates, verify statistical choices, and confirm outputs before sharing.
:::

:::
